name: Recently Added
author: Quips Editorial
description: The 25 most recently added quotes across the Quips collections, excluding quotes introduced with brand-new collections.

quotes.content: What I cannot create, I do not understand.
quotes.source: Blackboard in his Caltech office at the time of his death, February 1988; Caltech Archives and Special Collections
quotes.notes: Photographed on Feynman's blackboard after his death, alongside the line 'Know how to solve every problem that has been solved'; documented by Caltech Archives and cited by science historians.

quotes.content:
The old Lie: Dulce et decorum est
Pro patria mori.
quotes.source: Dulce et Decorum Est (written 1917, published in Poems, 1920)
quotes.notes: The closing indictment of Horace's maxim ('it is sweet and fitting to die for one's country'), drafted at Craiglockhart War Hospital and published posthumously.

quotes.content: As we leave the Moon at Taurus-Littrow, we leave as we came and, God willing, as we shall return, with peace and hope for all mankind.
quotes.source: Apollo 17, final words on the lunar surface (14 December 1972)
quotes.notes: Cernan's closing remarks as the last person to walk on the Moon, recorded in NASA's air-to-ground transcript.

quotes.content: I am alone now, truly alone, and absolutely isolated from any known life. I am it. If a count were taken, the score would be three billion plus two over on the other side of the moon, and one plus God knows what on this side.
quotes.source: Carrying the Fire: An Astronaut's Journey (1974)
quotes.notes: Collins's reflection on his roughly 48 minutes of solitude aboard the Apollo 11 command module, alone on the far side of the Moon during Armstrong and Aldrin's descent.

quotes.content:
I'm Nobody! Who are you?
Are you – Nobody – too?
quotes.notes: The poem's famous opening couplet; only these two lines are quoted since a later line has a disputed manuscript-vs-published wording.

quotes.content: Value investing is at its core the marriage of a contrarian streak and a calculator.
quotes.source: Margin of Safety: Risk-Averse Value Investing Strategies for the Thoughtful Investor (1991)
quotes.notes: Klarman's summary of the temperament value investing demands; the long-out-of-print book now sells for thousands of dollars used.

quotes.content: The three most harmful addictions are heroin, carbohydrates, and a monthly salary.
quotes.source: The Bed of Procrustes: Philosophical and Practical Aphorisms (2010)
quotes.notes: Often misattributed to Taleb's later Skin in the Game; the aphorism originates in this 2010 collection.

quotes.content: Experience is what you got when you didn't get what you wanted.
quotes.source: The Most Important Thing Illuminated: Uncommon Sense for the Thoughtful Investor (2013)
quotes.notes: Marks's maxim from the book's chapters on risk, on why hard markets teach the lessons easy ones cannot.

quotes.content: How do you know if you want something yourself or if your upbringing programmed you to want it?
quotes.notes: Trinity (as 'Tiffany') mulling free will vs. conditioning during a coffee-shop conversation with Thomas Anderson.

quotes.content: Where better to bury truth than inside something as ordinary as a video game?
quotes.notes: Bugs explaining how the Matrix weaponizes and trivializes important ideas by burying them in fiction.

quotes.content: Everything that has a beginning has an end, Neo.
quotes.notes: Smith recalling the Oracle's words during the climactic rain-soaked duel with Neo.

quotes.content: Choice is an illusion, created between those with power, and those without.
quotes.notes: The Merovingian's determinist counter-argument to Morpheus at Le Vrai restaurant.

quotes.content: Everything begins with choice.
quotes.notes: Morpheus's rebuttal to the Merovingian during their philosophical clash over free will.

quotes.content: Never send a human to do a machine's job.
quotes.notes: Smith's dismissive line just before ordering sentinels deployed instead of relying on human agents to hunt Morpheus's crew.

quotes.content: I can't let you go back unless you change your mind and build your summer home somewhere else.
quotes.notes: Pacha's leverage line while guiding llama-Kuzco through the jungle, refusing to help him home unless he spares the village.

quotes.content: Yeah. My family has lived on that hilltop for the last 6 generations.
quotes.notes: Pacha describing his home right before Kuzco reveals he plans to demolish it for his pool resort.

quotes.content: Yeah, weird.
quotes.notes: Kronk's flat response to Yzma's shock that Kuzco is now a talking llama instead of dead.

quotes.content: You know, in my defense, your poisons all look alike. You might think about relabeling some of them.
quotes.notes: Kronk's deadpan excuse after the poison mix-up turns Kuzco into a llama instead of killing him.

quotes.content: Oh, yeah. I thought we'd start off with soup and a light salad, and then see how we feel after that.
quotes.notes: Kronk's oblivious answer when Yzma asks if the poison is ready for dinner.

quotes.content: This isn't poison. This is extract of llama!
quotes.notes: Yzma realizing the assassination attempt on Kuzco used the wrong potion.

quotes.content: Why do we even have that lever?
quotes.notes: Yzma's exasperated complaint immediately after the wrong-lever fall.

quotes.content: Pull the lever, Kronk!
quotes.notes: Yzma's command en route to her secret lab — Kronk pulls the wrong one and she falls into the alligator pit.

quotes.content: Whoa! No touchy! No touchy. No touch.
quotes.notes: Kuzco recoiling when Kronk suggests testing whether Yzma is a robot.

quotes.content: This is Yzma, the emperor's advisor — living proof that dinosaurs once roamed the Earth.
quotes.notes: Kuzco's narrated introduction of Yzma early in the film.

quotes.content: You threw off my groove!
quotes.notes: Said after an old man bumps into Kuzco during his imperial strut, kicking off the film's running gag.
